intro:
Macedonia was a Roman province in the northern Aegean region — roughly the territory of modern northern Greece and the southern Balkans — lying north of Achaia and west of Thrace. It had been the home of Alexander the Great, whose conquests spread Greek culture across the eastern world. By the New Testament period it was organized as a Roman senatorial province with Thessalonica as its capital. Paul received the Macedonian call in a night vision at Troas (Acts 16:9–10), crossing from Asia into Europe to bring the gospel there for the first time.

Paul's ministry in Macedonia produced three significant churches: Philippi (Acts 16:11–40), Thessalonica (Acts 17:1–9), and Berea (Acts 17:10–14). He revisited the province multiple times (Acts 20:1–3; 2 Corinthians 2:13; 7:5) and held the Macedonian churches up as models of generosity, noting that despite their deep poverty they gave liberally to the collection for the Jerusalem poor (2 Corinthians 8:1–5; Romans 15:26). The two letters to the Thessalonians and the letter to the Philippians address Macedonian congregations directly.
